old_claim.note: Mechanical Theater Machinery provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: San Cassiano opera-house infrastructure used stage machinery and scene sets for magical effects, rapid scene changes, flying bodies, clouds, vehicles, and spectacle.

source_shape.source_locator: The page describes L'Andromeda's 1637 libretto recounting special effects fashioned by stage machinery and scene sets, including flying bodies, moving clouds, changing scenes, vehicles, and magical Baroque effects.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes stage machinery and scene sets as part of the 1637 San Cassiano opera-house spectacle.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Stage machinery is an enabling production subsystem of the opera house, but the venue can still be scoped more broadly than machinery alone.

ontology_before: Opera House Infrastructure depended on Mechanical Theater Machinery with an unsourced generic enabling claim.
ontology_after: Opera House Infrastructure keeps Mechanical Theater Machinery as a source-backed production subsystem for effects and scenery.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: mechanical_theater_machinery remains a direct enabling prerequisite with review evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to box-office or patronage operations with no staged scenic production.
A stronger source showed early public opera houses lacked stage machinery or scene-set infrastructure.
Mechanical Theater Machinery were rescoped away from stage effects, scenery, or movable devices.
short_reason: San Cassiano's public opera spectacle explicitly used stage machinery and scene sets.
